coredata.dc:title: Investigation of nitrogen-doped carbon dot/ZnO nanocomposites and their application as interlayer in solution-processed organic light emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: de Morais, Andreia
de Souza Rodrigues, Wesley
Coutinho, Douglas José
Nogueira, Ana Flávia
de Freitas, Jilian Nei
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CD) were synthesized and added to ZnO dispersions. The resulting composites were deposited between an emissive poly(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl) (PFO) layer and the top metal electrode of organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs). The structural, morphological and optical properties of N-CD sample and composite films were investigated. In solid-state, the N-CD presented a highly anisotropic behaviour, with significant size change, as demonstrated in microscopic images. Nevertheless, it was found that small amounts of N-CD reduced the surface roughness of the ZnO films and facilitated electron injection from the electron transport layer (ETL) to the polymeric light-emitting layer, thus demonstrating that ZnO/N-CD composites with appropriate ratios can be successfully used as ETL to boost the performance of OLEDs. All the OLEDs investigated emitted the blue colour characteristic of PFO, with minor shifts in the chromaticity coordinates, suggesting that the N-CD did not play a significant role as light-emitters in these devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
ZnO, nanocomposite
Solution-processing
LED
OLED

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the ZnO and ZnO/N-CD films

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Inovação
Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico
Centro Nacional de Pesquisa em Energia e Materiais

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge PCI/CTI/CNPq (313069/2016-6; 301118/2021-3) and CNPq for scholarships and financial support. Research supported by CTI-Nano, strategic laboratory from SisNano, MCTI and financed by CNPq. The authors thank LNNano/LNLS at CNPEM for the support in XPS measurements, AFM and KPFM images.

The authors acknowledge PCI/CTI/CNPq (313069/2016-6; 301118/2021-3) and CNPq for scholarships and financial support. Research supported by CTI-Nano, strategic laboratory from SisNano, MCTI and financed by CNPq. The authors thank LNNano/LNLS at CNPEM for the support in XPS measurements, AFM and KPFM images.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (Left) OLED configuration: glass-ITO|PEDOT:PSS|PVK|PFO|ZnO/N-CD|Ca|Al. (Right) Chemical structures of PFO and PVK.
(a) XRD, (b) FTIR spectrum and (c) representation of the chemical structure of the N-CD.

(a) AFM image of the N-CD deposited by spin-coating on a silicon substrate. The scale bar indicates 20 μm. (b) Height profiles collected in the points 1, 2 and 3; and (c,d) histograms of size-distributions of the N-CD, estimated from the AFM image (height corresponds to the dimensions displayed in the z axis; diameter correspond to the dimensions observed in the x,y axis).

(a) UV–visible absorption (blue line) and PL emission spectra (λexc = 350–490 nm) (green lines) of the N-CD dispersed in deionized water. The inset shows images of the N-CD dispersed in deionized water under irradiation of visible light (left) and UV light (365 nm) (right). (b) Excitation spectra of the N-CD dispersed in deionized water (λem = 500 and 530 nm) (red lines). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

AFM images of the OLEDs containing (a) ZnO; (b) ZnO/N-CD(0.1%); (c) ZnO/N-CD(0.25%); (d) ZnO/N-CD(0.5%); (e) ZnO/N-CD(1%) and (f) ZnO/N-CD(2%) films as ETL. The scanning area was 50 μm × 50 μm in all images.

(a) Current density–voltage curves (black) and luminance-voltage curves (colored); (b) current efficiency as a function of applied voltage and EL spectra (at 8.0 V) for the OLEDs: glass-ITO | PEDOT:PSS | PVK | PFO | ZnO or ZnO/N-CD | Ca | Al. The concentration of the N-CD in the ZnO matrix was varied from 0.1 to 2 wt%.

(a) Log J vs. log V plots for the electron-only devices: glass-ITO|PFO| ZnO or ZnO/N-CD |Ca|Al. The concentration of N-CD in the ZnO matrix was varied from 0.1 to 2 wt%. The inset in (a) shows log J vs. log V plot for Mott-Gurney SCLC fitting of the glass-ITO|PFO|ZnO|Ca|Al device. (b) Calculated average electron mobility (μe) within electron-only devices containing ZnO or ZnO/N-CD(0.1 to 2%) as ETL, using 4 samples for each condition. Each value was obtained from the Mott-Gurney SCLC equation.

Diagram illustrating the frontier energy levels of the N-CD, estimated from cyclic voltammetry measurements, and energy levels of the other diode components (ITO, PEDOT:PSS, PVK, PFO, ZnO, Ca and Al), extracted from the literature [21,68,70,71,72].
Parameters obtained for OLEDs containing ZnO or ZnO/N-CD as ETL and PFO as blue-emitting material. The concentration of the N-CD in the ZnO matrix was varied from 0.1 to 2 wt%.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Values of CIE 1931 were obtained at the maximum current efficiency (η curr).

head.ce:title: Investigation of nitrogen-doped carbon dot/ZnO nanocomposites and their application as interlayer in solution-processed organic light emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Information Technology Renato Archer (CTI), Rodovia D. Pedro I, Km 143,6, 13069-901 Campinas, SP, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Information Technology Renato Archer (CTI)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Information Technology Renato Archer (CTI), Rodovia D. Pedro I, Km 143,6, 13069-901 Campinas, SP, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Institute, University of Campinas (Unicamp), PO Box 6154, 13083-970 Campinas, SP, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Institute, University of Campinas (Unicamp), PO Box 6154, 13083-970 Campinas, SP, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Techological Federal University of Paraná (UTFPR), Rua Cristo Rei, n. 19, 85902-490 Toledo, PR, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Techological Federal University of Paraná (UTFPR), Rua Cristo Rei, n. 19, 85902-490 Toledo, PR, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CD) were synthesized and added to ZnO dispersions. The resulting composites were deposited between an emissive poly(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl) (PFO) layer and the top metal electrode of organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs). The structural, morphological and optical properties of N-CD sample and composite films were investigated. In solid-state, the N-CD presented a highly anisotropic behaviour, with significant size change, as demonstrated in microscopic images. Nevertheless, it was found that small amounts of N-CD reduced the surface roughness of the ZnO films and facilitated electron injection from the electron transport layer (ETL) to the polymeric light-emitting layer, thus demonstrating that ZnO/N-CD composites with appropriate ratios can be successfully used as ETL to boost the performance of OLEDs. All the OLEDs investigated emitted the blue colour characteristic of PFO, with minor shifts in the chromaticity coordinates, suggesting that the N-CD did not play a significant role as light-emitters in these devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CD), discovered in 2004 by Xu et al. [1], are quasi-spherical structures containing carbon atoms with sp2 and sp3 hybridization, and hydrophilic functional groups on their surface [2]. These materials exhibit low toxicity, high solubility in water and in some organic solvents, and tunable electronic and optical properties [2,3]. Heteroatom-doping [4,5,6], surface modification [7] and passivation with organic molecules [8] are strategies used to further enhance CD properties. Among them, nitrogen-doping is one of the most studied methods because it is facile and effective. Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CD) usually present high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) with emissions centered in the blue or green spectral regions [9,10], but other multicolored-emissive N-CD have also been prepared with this approach [7,11,12].

Due to their versatility, CD are widely investigated for application in optoelectronic devices [13,14], including their use as photosensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells [15], or as electron or hole accepting materials in organic [16] and perovskite solar cells [17]. In recent years, their application as light-emitting layer [18,19,20], emitting layer dopant [11,21,22,23], electron transport layer (ETL) [24,25,26], hole transport layer (HTL) [27,28,29], or interfacial modification layer [30] in organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) have also been reported [31].

Concomitantly, the use of solution processing techniques to prepare thin films has been considered attractive for low-cost and large-scale device fabrication. ZnO, for example, is one of the most widely used solution-processable inorganic n-type semiconductors as ETL in OLEDs [32,33]. This material has a high electronic mobility, high exciton binding energy, a wide energy band gap (Eg = 3.20 eV), and can be easily prepared from inexpensive routes. Considering its high valence band level (EVB = 7.6 eV), the ZnO film can effectively block hole diffusion towards the cathode, also acting as an efficient hole blocking layer (HBL) in OLEDs [34]. However, ZnO nanoparticles have a significant number of surface defects such as oxygen vacancy that may act as electron trap sites [35]. There is also a high electron injection barrier from the conduction band of ZnO to the LUMO level of several materials typically used as light-emitting layer [36]. Thus, various interlayers [33,37] and modifiers [35,38] have been used to tune the energy level alignment between ZnO and organic emissive layers, including, for example, carbon nanotube [39] and graphene sheets [40].

Although experimental reports involving the use of ZnO/CD composites in organic electronic devices are available in the literature [41,42], so far there have been only a few reports regarding their application as ETL in OLEDs. For example, Zubair et al. [24] reported the use of ZnO/graphene quantum dots films as ETL in red light-emitting OLEDs with configuration glass-ITO|PEDOT:PSS|MEH-PPV|ZnO/graphene quantum dots|Al. This diode exhibited a reduced turn on voltage (VON) and a four-fold current density increase compared to the standard device without CD. In another work, Zhang et al. [26] proposed the use of CD as interface modifiers between ZnO films and active layers of CsPbI3 quantum dot-LEDs (QLEDs) and CdSe/ZnS QLEDs. They reported that the CD minimize the charge injection/extraction energy barrier, improving the interface contact in these devices. Overall, the use of CD as unique material for the ETL, or as surface modifier for ZnO by deposition of a CD overlayer on top of a ZnO film, has been more explored in the preparation of the ETL in organic devices [25,39,41,42] than the use of pre-mixed ZnO/CD composites, which has been less explored so far.

Herein, we investigate the properties of solution-processable ZnO/N-CD composites and their use as ETL in blue-emitting OLEDs with the configuration: glass-ITO|PEDOT:PSS|PVK|PFO|ZnO/N-CD|Ca|Al. The N-CD were prepared by solid-phase thermal treatment using citric acid and dicyandiamide as precursors, and ZnO was prepared by a sol–gel method. The effect of incorporation of different amounts of N-CD (guest) in the ZnO matrix (host) was investigated, varying the guest concentration from 0.1 to 2 wt%. The structural, morphological and optical properties of the N-CD sample and composite films were investigated to demonstrate how the concentration and dispersion of N-CD into the ZnO films affect the final performance of the OLED. To our knowledge, this is the first report dedicated to investigate the effect of varying the composition of ZnO/N-CD composites as ETL in a blue-emitting PFO-based OLED with the proposed configuration.

Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) was purchased from H.C. Starck (Clevios P VP AI 4083). Poly[9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl]-end capped with dimethylphenyl (PFO) (Mw = 40,000 – 150,000 g mol−1) was purchased from American Dye Source (ADS129BE). Poly(9-vinylcarbazole) (PVK) (Mw = 1,100,000 g mol−1) and the other chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All solvents were anhydrous or analytical grade and used without further purification. The indium-doped tin oxide (ITO) conductive glasses (Corning Eagle XG/1737, 7–10 ohm sq-1) were cut, patterned by photolithography, cleaned in acid solution, rinsed thoroughly with deionized water and dried under nitrogen flow. An oxygen plasma treatment (Eni Power Systems, Model ACG-5) was performed with an oxygen flow of 950 dm3 min−1 for 5 min prior to use.

N-CD were prepared by one-pot, solid-phase thermal treatment [43]. Firstly, a mixture of 1.8 g of citric acid and 1.2 g of dicyandiamide was heated in a muffle furnace at 230 °C for 90 min under continuous nitrogen flux. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting material was dispersed in ethanol with the aid of an ultrasonic bath. The removal of large particles was performed by centrifugation (10,000 rpm for 15 min) and vacuum filtration through a porous PTFE membrane filter (Millipore®, 0.22 µm). Finally, the solvent was evaporated and a dry final product was obtained.

Zinc acetate dihydrate (0.2 g) was dissolved in methanol (8.5 mL) upon stirring at 65 °C for 30 min. A solution of potassium hydroxide (0.1 g) in methanol (5.5 mL) was then added dropwise at 65 °C over a period of 15 min and the resulting reaction mixture was kept under stirring at 65 °C for 2.5 h. The dispersion color becomes opaque after the reaction. After cooling down to room temperature, the dispersion was centrifuged (10,000 rpm for 5 min) and the precipitate was washed twice with methanol.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was carried out in a Shimadzu XRD7000 diffractometer (40 kV, 30 mA) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54178 Å) in the 2θ range of 5-80°. Thermogravimetric (TGA) curve was obtained on a TA Instruments 2950 thermogravimetric analyzer at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1, in the temperature range of 25 to 900 °C, under synthetic air stream (100 dm3 min−1). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Agilent CARY 630 FTIR) was carried out in the range of 400 and 4000 cm−1. Elemental analysis was performed using a Perkin Elmer CHN2400 microanalyzer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyzes were performed using an X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific) under a reduced pressure of 10-10 mbar, using a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) as the excitation source. Data analysis was performed in CasaXPS, setting the C1s peak at 284.8 eV as the reference for calibration.

The redox potentials of the N-CD were estimated from cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements carried out in a conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell, using an Autolab® PGSTAT 10 N potentiostat (Eco Chemie). Glass-FTO|platinum film was used as working electrode, with platinum wire as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl (3.0 mol/L LiCl in ethanol) as reference electrode in a 0.1 mol/L solution of tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate in dimethylformamide (DMF). The scan rate was 20 mV s−1 and the Fc+/Fc redox couple in DMF was used as an internal standard. The HOMO (EHOMO) energy was calculated with Eq. (1) [46] and the bandgap (Eg) was determined from the absorption spectra onset. Then, LUMO energy (ELUMO) was calculated by combining the values of EHOMO and Eg.
(1)
E
HOMO
= -
E
o x i d , o n s e t v s .
Fc
+
/ F c
+ 4.8
( e V )

Optical microscopy images of the ZnO and ZnO/N-CD films were taken with an Olympus B51x microscope (Olympus Corp., USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the same films were obtained with a Field Emission Gun Scanning Electron Microscope (FEG-SEM) MIRA3 XMU (Tescan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were acquired with Nanosurf, EasysScan 2 microscope, operating in the tapping mode. Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) images of the ZnO and ZnO/N-CD(0.1 or 1 wt%) films deposited over 1 cm × 1 cm substrates were acquired with a PARK SYSTEMS (NX-10) microscope operating in the tapping mode and using standard metallized tips (PPP-EFM, Nanosensors; nominal force constant 2.8 N m−1 and nominal resonance frequency ∼75 kHz) that consisted of silicon probes with an overall metallic coating of Pt/Ir on both sides of the cantilever.

The electronic absorption spectrum of the N-CD in deionized water was collected with an Agilent Cary 60 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer. Steady-state fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Cary Eclipse Varian spectrofluorimeter, varying the excitation wavelength (λexc) from 350 to 490 nm, and the emission wavelength (λem) at 500 and 530 nm. PLQY was measured using Coumarin 153 dye in ethanol as standard sample. Absolute values were calculated according to Eq. (2):
(2)
ϕ
N - C D s
=
ϕ
C 153
α
N - C D s
α
C 153
.
η
N - C D s
η
C 153
where ϕ is the quantum yield, α is the slope of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance and η is the refractive index (η H2O = 1.33 and η ethanol = 1.36).

OLEDs with the configuration glass-ITO|PEDOT:PSS|PVK|PFO|ZnO or ZnO/N-CD|Ca|Al were assembled. First, the PEDOT:PSS dispersion was filtered through a PVDF membrane filter (Millipore®, 0.45 μm) layer and deposited by spin-coating (3000 rpm, 40 s) onto a pre-patterned ITO-glass substrate, then dried at 120 °C for 20 min. A layer of PVK (10 mg mL−1 in THF) was spin-coated (4000 rpm, 40 s) on top of the PEDOT:PSS layer. Then, the light-emitting layer was spin-coated (2000 rpm, 40 s) from a chlorobenzene solution of PFO (10 mg mL−1). These solutions were filtered through a PTFE membrane filter (Millipore®, 0.45 μm) immediately before deposition. Subsequently, the ZnO or ZnO/N-CD film was deposited by spin-coating (5000 rpm, 40 s) on top the PFO layer. The films were placed on a hot plate at 100 °C for 10 min. Finally, Ca (20 nm) and Al (80 nm) were thermally evaporated in an MBraun-Evaporator glovebox (pressure of ∼ 5 × 10−6 mbar). The configuration of the OLEDs and the chemical structures of PFO and PVK are displayed in Fig. 1 .

After assembly, the diodes were placed inside a hermetically sealed capsule and removed from the glovebox for characterization. Electroluminescence (EL) spectra were acquired with an Ocean Optics USB2000 + fluorimeter. The luminance and CIE coordinates were obtained with a CS-100A Color and Luminance Meter (Konica Minolta), whereas the current–voltage (J-V) curves were obtained with a Keithley 2410-C source meter.

The crystalline structure of the N-CD was investigated by XRD, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The XRD pattern of the N-CD powder exhibits two diffraction peaks at 10.8 and 27.3, corresponding to the (001) and (002) planes of graphitic framework, respectively. The diffraction peak at 2 θ = 10.8, with an interplanar spacing of d(001) = 8.18 Å, was attributed to an increase in sp3 layers spacing. During the pyrolysis process, oxygen-containing and amino functional groups are generated and bonded to the edges of basal planes of the graphitic structure, resulting in an increase in the interlayer spacing [47,48]. The second diffraction peak, at 2 θ = 27.3 (d(002) = 3.26 Å), is similar to the typical diffraction peak of graphite at 2 θ = 26.6° (d(002) = 3.35 Å) [49]. However, with an upward shift, the peak becomes weak and broad, indicating a highly disordered carbon structure with a decrease in sp2 layer spacing due to the carbonization process. This result suggests that the N-CD with poor crystalline nature possess a heterogeneous multilayered structure, similar to the findings of other reports in the literature [47,48].

The functional groups on the surface of the N-CD powder were identified through the FTIR spectrum. As shown in Fig. 2b, the N-CD exhibited characteristic broad absorption bands of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations of amine groups at around 3180 cm−1. The bands at 2750 and 2650 cm−1 correspond to the C–H stretching vibrations. The bands at around 1610 and 1540 cm−1 are assigned to the typical stretching vibrations of the C=N and N–H bonds, respectively. The stretching vibration bands of C=O, C=C and C–O are exhibited at 1690, 1540 and 1180 cm−1, respectively [5,50].

In short, the characterizations showed that the as-synthesized N-CD are quasi-spherical particles (diameter ca. 50 nm) consisting of amorphous and crystalline parts, containing carbon atoms with sp2 and sp3 hybridization, and a surface rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. These structural features make them highly soluble and stable in water and some organic solvents (e.g., DMSO and DMF). Furthermore, these N-CD show strong tendency to self-organize in large agglomerates with anisotropic features depending on the processing conditions (i.e., round shaped, with ca. 100 nm of height and dimensions > 1 µm in length - parallel position in relation to the substrate). The significant difference found in the particle/aggregate dimensions is expected to impact on their application in the OLEDs. As will be demonstrated, the large aggregates seem to be formed in the ZnO/N-CD composite films as well. Thus, the orientation in which these particles self-organize in relation to the substrate surface, with a much lower height than length, is very important and enables their use in the OLEDs investigated here, as each layer of the diode must be comprised of thin films on the order of nanometers.

The UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence (PL) (λexc = 350–490 nm), and excitation spectra (λem = 500 and 530 nm) of the N-CD dispersed in deionized water are displayed in Fig. 4 . The N-CD exhibited two characteristic absorption bands around 260 and 405 nm (Fig. 4a, blue line), assigned to π–π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains and n–π transitions by the surface states, respectively [43,54,55]. As can be seen in the inset of Fig. 4a, the N-CD show a strong greenish emission under irradiation of ultraviolet light. The PL spectrum (λexc = 410 nm) of the N-CD (Fig. 4a, green line) showed two broad and partially overlapping fluorescence bands, centered at 500 and 530 nm, respectively, with full width at half maximum (FWHM) around 118 nm. When the excitation wavelength (λexc) was increased from 350 to 490 nm in increments of 10 nm, the emission signal displayed a little red shift, from 528 to 548 nm. An excitation-dependent PL behavior is common in fluorescent carbon materials [55,56,57] and is usually associated with the non-uniform particle size and distribution of different surface states [58]. In addition, the PLQY of the N-CD dispersed in deionized water at room temperature was found to be 16 %, using Coumarin 153 dye (ethanol as solvent; PLQY = 53 %) as a reference (see Fig. S7, Supplementary Information ). The excitation spectra (λem = 500 or 530 nm) of the N-CD exhibited a maximum broad fluorescence excitation band at 410 nm, which matches with the optical absorption band (see Fig. 4, blue and red lines).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the ZnO and ZnO/N-CD films

ce:section.ce:para: OLEDs containing the ZnO or ZnO/N-CD films as ETL were assembled with the following configuration: glass-ITO|PEDOT:PSS|PVK|PFO|ZnO or ZnO/N-CD|Ca|Al, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The concentration of N-CD in the ZnO matrix was varied from 0.1 to 2 wt%. The current–voltage, luminance-voltage, current efficiency–voltage curves, and EL spectra are exhibited in Fig. 6 . The data extracted from current density (J), luminance (L), current efficiency (η curr) curves as a function of the applied voltage, and the chromaticity coordinates (x,y, CIE 1931) are summarized in Table 1 .

To further investigate the effect of the addition of N-CD into ZnO in the composite ETL, J-V curves were obtained for electron-only devices assembled with the configuration glass-ITO|PFO|ZnO or ZnO/N-CD(0.1 to 2%)|Ca|Al (see Fig. 7 a). The electron mobility was calculated using the space-charge limited current (SCLC) model according to the Mott-Gurney law [65]. The model follows the relation expressed by Eq. (3):
(3)
J =
9 8
μ e
ε 0
ε r
V
2
l
3
where J is the current density, μe is the electron mobility,
ε 0
is the free-space permittivity (
ε 0
= 8.85x10-14F cm−1),
ε r
is the material dielectric constant (
ε
PFO
= 3), V is the applied voltage, and
l
is the active layer thickness (
l
= 38 nm), which was measured using a profilometer. The J-V curves of the electron-only devices were fitted with the SCLC model. The data adjustment was performed in the region where the relation between J and V is quadratic (J α V2), as shown in the inset in Fig. 7a.

The average μe was calculated to be 1.2x10-6 cm2 V−1 s−1 for the electron-only device without N-CD. The electron-only devices containing ZnO/N-CD films with 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1 and 2% of N-CD presented average μe values of 1.3x10-6 cm2 V−1 s−1, 1.3x10-6 cm2 V−1 s−1, 1.7x10-6 cm2 V−1 s−1, 2.2x10-6 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 0.9x10-6 cm2 V−1 s−1, respectively. Thus, only a minor change was observed in the average μe value with increasing the amount of N-CD in the ZnO/N-CD composite ETL, remaining in the same order of magnitude. These μe values are compatible with the literature [66,67]. From this result, it can be inferred that a contribution to enhance charge transport is not the main effect that arises from the addition of N-CD.

Then, to explain the role of the N-CD, we illustrated a diagram of the energy levels of the components of the diode in Fig. 8 . The energy levels of ITO [68], PEDOT:PSS [68], PVK [69,70], PFO [70], Ca [71] and Al [71] were obtained from the literature. The EHOMO and ELUMO of the N-CD estimated from CV measurements, as previously discussed, were −5.05 eV and −2.43 eV, respectively. It is important to mention that the energy levels illustrated for the ETL in Fig. 8 are based on values estimated for the individual materials rather than from a measurement collected directly in the composites. We tried to access this information by performing cyclic voltammetry measurements in ZnO/N-CD films deposited onto FTO substrates, but it was not possible to see a clear signal with this method. Since this ETL is based on a physical mixture of the materials, it is possible that the energy levels of each individual component remain approximately the same in the composite, so we assume the levels illustrated in Fig. 8 for ZnO and N-CD as a valid approach for an initial analysis. Nonetheless, it should be kept in mind that, as reported by Zhang et al. [72], the agglomeration effect in CD samples could lead to some level of band gap decrease (more or less pronounced, depending on the CD sample), which would change the energy levels of these materials.

Considering the approximation of using the energy levels of each individual material as a valid starting point here, according to Fig. 8, the insertion of N-CD is expected to reduce the energy barrier and facilitate the electron injection from the ETL (ZnO/N-CD film) to the emissive layer (PFO) by providing an alternative path for charge transfer, i.e., either a direct injection from the electrode to the carbon dot phase, or injection from the conduction band of ZnO to the LUMO level of the N-CD, then to PFO (in a cascade mechanism). From KPFM images, we observed that the contact potential difference (CPD) had a less negative value for the ZnO/N-CD sample, indicating a shift of the surface work function to lower values, that is, an increase in the number of electron carriers, possibly because of this extra path for charge transfer. Furthermore, the smoother interface that is formed with the Ca/Al contact due to the decrease of surface roughness observed upon addition of small amounts of N-CD (see AFM images in Fig. 5) also contributes to facilitate electron injection and balance charge carrier transport. Tuning the ETL composition has been a widely used strategy to introduce changes in the energy levels and in band alignment, being a means to decrease the operation voltage in OLED [73]. Here, the addition of small amounts of N-CD to the ETL contributed to the lower the operating voltage, with VON reducing from 4.0 V to 3.6 V and 3.8 V for the diodes prepared with ZnO or ZnO/N-CD(0.1%) and ZnO/N-CD(0.25%), respectively. On the other hand, for the samples containing higher concentrations of N-CD, VON is increased, reaching 4.8 V for the diode containing ZnO/N-CD(2%). As seen from optical microscopy and AFM images, at higher concentrations there is the formation of larger N-CD aggregates inside the ZnO film, with an increase in surface roughness. This could lead to two effects: i) increased phase separation within the composite; or ii) formation of a worst contact (coarser) between the top electrode and the ETL. In either case it could lead to formation of higher energetic barriers and formation of traps, thus increasing VON.

Therefore, the main impact expected from adding different amounts of N-CD to the composite is in the formation of a larger phase of this material inside the ZnO matrix, meaning extended pathways for electrons to be transferred. In addition to the effects discussed here, the superficial functional groups present on the N-CD might passivate the surface defects of ZnO nanoparticles, reducing recombination centers and traps at the inorganic phase, as reported in other works [26,41,42].

Overall, according to the results obtained here, the use of solution-processed ZnO/N-CD films might be an interesting alternative to improve the efficiency of PFO-based OLED. However, the concentration and dispersion of the N-CD in the ZnO films must be carefully adjusted to avoid formation of larger aggregates, which directly affect the final performance of these devices. In general, CDs in the solid state suffer aggregation due to strong intermolecular π-π stacking interactions, which may be avoided by introducing steric hindrance. For example, Xu et al. [21] reported that surface functionalization of CDs with hexadecylamine agent could reduce the π-π stacking. These nanoparticles were then dispersed in a PVK matrix, which is also another strategy to avoid aggregation (i.e, enhancing the chemical compatibility between the host and the matrix). In fact, several parameters could be manipulated to tune aggregation, such as the type of solvent, type of matrix, surface functional groups, and use of bulky surface-passivation molecules [74]. We believed that the introduction of some stabilizing agent (e.g. acetylacetone) during the preparation of ZnO/N-CDs suspension could be a path to reduce aggregation and enhance the dispersion of larger concentrations of N-CD in our composites in future works. However, is important to consider that the addition of surfactants in the precursor ink, or use of bulky molecules as surface passivators, might hinder the charge transport processes in the film, as these molecules are often insulators, making it tricky to reach a balance between preventing aggregation and keeping the desired electronic properties.

N-CD were synthesized by solid-phase pyrolysis and used in the preparation of composites with ZnO. The N-CD presented a surface rich in functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen, and under irradiation of ultraviolet light, emitted a greenish color with 16% PLQY. The morphological aspects were investigated by microscopy techniques. Structures with major variation of the dimensions were observed depending on the processing conditions: quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes less than 50 nm were observed in transmission-mode SEM images, while the formation of large aggregates (>1 µm) were seen in AFM images, showing that the organization of these materials was very susceptible to the processing conditions. Regardless of the dimensions, a round shape was always observed in the direction parallel to the substrate surface. After characterization, different concentrations of N-CD (0.1 to 2 wt%) were mixed with ZnO and the resulting composites were applied as ETL in OLEDs. All the OLEDs emitted the blue color characteristic of PFO, with little shift in the chromaticity coordinates, indicating that the N-CDs did not play a significant role as light-emitters in these diodes. The use of the ZnO/N-CD(0.1%) film as ETL improved significantly the performance of the device, reaching a maximum luminance of 581 cd m−2 at 7.6 V. In this case, the N-CD act as passivators for the ZnO nanoparticles, smoothing the surface, as seen in AFM images, and facilitating the electron injection from the ZnO/N-CD ETL to the PFO light-emitting layer. On the other hand, at higher concentrations (N-CD > 0.5 wt%), the diodes delivered lower performances that were related to the formation of larger N-CD aggregates inside the ZnO film. Thus, the concentration and dispersion of N-CD are two important parameters that determined the final performance of the PFO-based OLED.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge PCI/CTI/CNPq (313069/2016-6; 301118/2021-3) and CNPq for scholarships and financial support. Research supported by CTI-Nano, strategic laboratory from SisNano, MCTI and financed by CNPq. The authors thank LNNano/LNLS at CNPEM for the support in XPS measurements, AFM and KPFM images.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2023.116749.
